topic: old Kensington stories — block parties, stickball

turns.content: Sitting here on the front step and the neighbors have a block party going. Took me right back.
Block parties have a way of doing that. What did it take you back to?
Growing up in Kensington in the 80s. Every summer the whole block would shut down. Somebody would get a permit, somebody else would hook up a fire hydrant. Kids everywhere. It was a different world.
The fire hydrant in summer is such a classic Philly image. Was your block tight-knit, or was it more of a every-family-for-themselves situation that came together for the party?

Tight. Everybody knew everybody. Mrs. Callahan would bring out a folding table with about 40 pounds of potato salad. My old man would be on the grill. The Delgado kids and us would play stickball in the street until it got dark. Nobody locked their doors.
Stickball in the street until dark — that's basically a lost art at this point. Were you any good, or are you about to tell me you were the best player on the block?

Listen I was decent. My brother had the arm. I could hit though. We used a broom handle and a Spalding. If you hit it past the Callahans' stoop that was a home run. Anything on the roof was an automatic out because nobody was going up there to get it.
The stoop and the roof as fair and foul territory — you didn't need umpires, you just needed landmarks. How many balls ended up on that roof over a summer?

Dozens. Mr. Callahan would go up with a bucket at the end of the summer and throw them all back down. He'd yell at us every time one went up there but he never actually got mad. Good man. Passed about ten years ago.

Sounds like the kind of guy every block needed — gruff about it but ultimately part of the whole thing. Those neighborhoods where the older generation kept things running and the kids had the run of the street — that's a specific era that's hard to explain to people who didn't live it. Do you ever drive through Kensington now?

I do. It's rough. I'm not going to get into it but it's not the same place. The block I grew up on still has some of the old families but most of them moved. I don't get sentimental about much but that one gets me a little.

That makes sense. Watching a place you know that well change that much — especially when you can still see the exact stoop and the exact street where the Spalding used to bounce — that's a hard thing to reconcile. The memories are still sharp even if the block isn't the same.
Yeah. Anyway. That's what a block party does to me apparently. Turns me into an old man sitting on the step telling stories.
Nothing wrong with that. Sounds like it was a good block and a good time. Enjoy the party tonight — even if the stickball standards have probably declined.
Copy that. They don't even know what a Spalding is. Good talk.
